picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet has held the #1 growth book slot every day this week and the thesis is firmly intact. Q1 2026 EPS of $5.11 crushed the $2.62 estimate; Google Cloud accelerated to +63% YoY growth, and Gemini Enterprise paid MAUs grew 40% QoQ — the AI monetization ramp is now visible in the income statement, not just narrative. Berkshire added GOOGL at +204% in Q1 2026, Goldman Sachs maintained it as a major holding, and Ackman (while rotating to MSFT) explicitly called GOOGL a long-term buy — the institutional conviction overlap is the deepest in the universe.
picks.catalyst: Google I/O 2026 Gemini 3.5 Flash launch (confirmed live May 19) and next Cloud revenue quarter (Q2 earnings ~late July)
picks.entryNote: Stock has climbed ~30% over the past six months; still trades at a discount to peers on cloud growth-adjusted basis. Consolidating constructively following the Q1 beat. No technical extension flag.
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud revenue growth decelerates below 40% YoY, or AI search cannibalization by ChatGPT/Perplexity materially erodes query share as evidenced by two consecutive quarters of search revenue miss
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory breakup of Google Search or ad business via DOJ antitrust proceedings

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom has been a top-2 growth book name all week with zero thesis deterioration. Druckenmiller established a large new position in AVGO (195,955 shares) as his primary AI infrastructure bet, rotating away from hyperscalers toward the custom silicon layer — the picks-and-shovels logic is compelling. Broadcom's XPU custom ASIC business for Google, Meta, and Apple is structurally differentiated, VMware integration has expanded operating margins, and the stock is up ~139% over the past year with positive estimate revisions on AI tailwinds. Ameriprise's +6% add in Q1 corroborates accumulation.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2026 earnings expected early June with hyperscaler custom silicon pipeline update
picks.entryNote: Mild extension flag noted given the 12-month +139% run and rich valuation, but Druckenmiller's fresh entry and persistent RS leadership justify holding. Near all-time highs but earnings revisions are accelerating upward.
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of a major hyperscaler ASIC design win (Google TPU or Meta MTIA shift away from Broadcom), or operating margin compression below 60% post-VMware
picks.keyRisk: Valuation compression if AI capex cycle peaks and hyperscaler custom silicon orders decelerate

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA reported Q1 FY2027 earnings today (May 20) — EPS of $1.87 beat the $1.76 estimate by 6.25%, revenue surged 85.2% YoY to $81.62B, and next-quarter guidance of $91B midpoint came in 5.7% above consensus. This is its 8th consecutive EPS beat. The Vera Rubin server cycle and Data Center revenue of $75.2B cement NVDA's irreplaceable position in AI infrastructure. The stock is trading ~$221-223 today, still ~6% below its May 14 all-time high of $236.54 — a post-earnings entry is structurally cleaner than a pre-earnings chase.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings (August 26, 2026) with Vera Rubin ramp revenue recognition and updated $91B+ guidance confirmation
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$221-223, down from ATH of $236.54; post-earnings digestion is creating a more constructive entry vs. pre-earnings chase. An analyst initiated Buy today with $300 price target. EBITDA margin of 61.7% is exceptional quality.
picks.exitTrigger: Data Center revenue growth decelerates below 50% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or a hyperscaler signals material AI capex reduction in earnings call
picks.keyRisk: Sell-the-news pattern on earnings day extends into technical breakdown below $200 support; export control escalation vs. China limits Hopper/Blackwell addressable market

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX has been a top-5 growth name every single day this week — the defense thesis is structural and accelerating. The Trump administration's push toward a $1.5 trillion defense budget, the ongoing Iran conflict keeping Hormuz tensions elevated, and NATO allies rearming create a multi-year backlog expansion cycle for RTX's Pratt & Whitney engines and Raytheon missile/defense systems. Unlike pure-play defense names, RTX has a commercial aerospace aftermarket revenue stream (high-margin MRO) that diversifies its earnings quality and insulates it from pure government budget risk.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected late July) with Pratt & Whitney GTF engine backlog update and Raytheon missile defense order flow from NATO partners
picks.entryNote: Trading at a reasonable P/E relative to aerospace/defense peers; sector has outperformed YTD +12%+ driven by defense spending tailwinds. No extension flag — earnings revisions are broadly positive and valuation is fair-to-slightly-rich but justified by structural backlog.
picks.exitTrigger: Hormuz reopening triggers a deflationary shock that leads Congress to pare the defense budget, or Pratt & Whitney GTF engine groundings escalate materially from current levels
picks.keyRisk: GTF engine defect remediation costs spiral and compress aerospace margins, coinciding with a ceasefire-driven reduction in Raytheon missile demand

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM has been a top-5 growth/value pick all week, and the energy thesis has strengthened — XLE is now formally co-Tier 1 on relative strength alongside XLI, with energy up 20%+ YTD driven by Brent crude at ~$110/barrel amid the Hormuz closure. XOM offers the best balance sheet quality in upstream E&P (AAA-credit equivalent), a Permian Basin production growth profile, and Pioneer Energy integration synergies flowing through. As a short-duration, hard-asset equity with a 3.5%+ dividend yield, it is a natural hedge against the stagflation scenario where long-duration assets compress. Berkshire's large CVX position (despite trimming) signals value investors still own energy.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with Pioneer integration update and Permian volume guidance; any Hormuz escalation scenario is a near-term positive catalyst
picks.entryNote: Energy sector approaching 'extended' readings on relative rotation but XOM's quality balance sheet and dividend yield provide downside buffer vs. pure E&P names. Brent at $110 means current earnings estimates are still being revised upward.
picks.exitTrigger: Brent crude falls sustainably below $75/barrel (Hormuz reopening + demand destruction scenario), or Pioneer integration reveals reserve write-downs
picks.keyRisk: Geopolitical resolution in the Middle East triggers a $20-30/bbl crude decline, rapidly compressing earnings estimates and reversing the energy sector's RS leadership

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: GS has anchored the top-6 growth book all week as the best expression of the financial sector's two key tailwinds: a positively sloped yield curve (2s10s at +50-55bps) expanding NIM, and a recovering M&A and IPO cycle with the Figma IPO pipeline rebuilding. Goldman's capital markets franchise is uniquely levered to both rising rates and a dealmaking recovery. The sector scorecard rates XLF at Tier 2 overweight, and GS is the highest-quality capital markets expression within financials. XLF forward P/E at 14-15x is not stretched, and earnings revisions are positive.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) with trading revenue and investment banking fee pipeline; revival of M&A advisory fees as deal confidence returns
picks.entryNote: Trading at fair valuation (~12-13x forward earnings) vs. history; the yield curve steepening is a direct NIM tailwind. Goldman grew its 13F portfolio 6.4% to $735B in Q1, signaling institutional confidence in financials. No extension flag.
picks.exitTrigger: Credit cycle turns with HY OAS widening above 500bps indicating financial stress, or investment banking revenues disappoint for a third consecutive quarter on deal cancelations
picks.keyRisk: A stagflation-driven recession scenario compresses advisory fees and triggers significant loan loss provisions across the financial sector

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: LLY has held a top-6 slot all week, and Q1 2026 results materially strengthen the thesis: Q1 revenue surged 56% to $19.8B, FY2026 guidance was raised to $82-85B (up $2B at each end), and the FDA approved Foundayo (orforglipron) — the first oral daily GLP-1 for obesity. This is a category-expanding event: a pill format removes the injection barrier and broadens the addressable market. The CMS Medicare Bridge program extension capping out-of-pocket costs at $50/month starting July 2026 is an additional demand unlock. The prior risk report's valuation concern is real but the guidance raise and new product approvals provide fundamental justification for premium multiples.
picks.catalyst: Foundayo commercial ramp with Medicare reimbursement access starting July 1, 2026; retatrutide Phase 3 obesity data readout in H2 2026
picks.catalystWindow: Next 6 weeks (Medicare access), then H2 2026 for pipeline
picks.entryNote: Trading around $1,005 per share; analyst consensus target of $1,218 implies ~21% upside with Morgan Stanley at $1,344. At 36x trailing earnings, valuation is not cheap, but guidance raised and new product cycle just beginning. 23 of 30 analysts rate Buy.
picks.exitTrigger: Foundayo safety concerns escalate into FDA regulatory action beyond the single liver failure report, or realized GLP-1 pricing falls more than 20% ex-US as generics enter earlier than expected
picks.keyRisk: Novo Nordisk's oral semaglutide (pill Wegovy) gains faster market share than anticipated, compressing Foundayo pricing and blunting the new product cycle

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI has been a top-8 name all week and continues to be one of the cleanest quality compounders in the universe. S&P Global benefits from a positive rate/credit issuance environment: higher-for-longer rates drive bond issuance as corporations refinance at new rates, generating consistent ratings revenue growth. The Market Intelligence and Indices businesses provide durable, high-margin recurring revenue with minimal capital intensity. The company's pricing power, near-duopoly moat in credit ratings (alongside Moody's), and low cyclicality make it an ideal late-cycle quality anchor in the growth book.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with bond issuance volume update; any acceleration in corporate debt refinancing from rising rates is a direct revenue tailwind
picks.entryNote: Trades at a premium to the market (~28x forward) but justified by its near-duopoly moat, 30%+ operating margins, and secular growth in index-linked assets. No extension flag — the business compounds consistently through credit cycles.
picks.exitTrigger: Bond issuance volumes collapse by more than 30% YoY (deep recession scenario), or antitrust action against the ratings duopoly structure emerges from DOJ
picks.keyRisk: A sudden credit market freeze (HY OAS blowing out above 600bps) would sharply reduce rated issuance, the primary volume driver for ratings revenue

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: AMZN dropped from Tier 1 to position 9 versus prior days' top-3 given the FCF compression risk flagged in the risk review (trailing 12-month FCF fell to $1.2B from $25.9B on surging AI capex), but the thesis remains intact for a 1-5 year holder. Q1 delivered EPS of $2.78 vs. $1.64 estimate; AWS at $37.6B (+28% YoY, 37.7% operating margin) is the fastest-growing in 15 quarters. Tepper made AMZN his top position at ~$900M (+98%), and Ackman added +19%. The capex surge is intentional investment in AWS/AI capacity — not structural deterioration. AWS is the single largest cloud platform and will monetize this spend over a 3-5 year window.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 AWS revenue and operating income print (late July); any AWS operating margin expansion signal from Trainium AI chip adoption would be a major re-rating event
picks.entryNote: Amazon has outperformed the S&P 500 YTD; technical structure is constructive but FCF compression creates near-term multiple ceiling. Tepper's $900M position and Ackman's +19% add confirm smart money conviction at current levels.
picks.exitTrigger: AWS revenue growth decelerates below 20% YoY, or FCF remains negative for more than two consecutive quarters without management providing credible FCF recovery timeline
picks.keyRisk: The massive $44.2B Q1 capex pace continues without commensurate AWS margin expansion, leading the market to reprice the FCF generation timeline downward

picks.name: Vistra Corp
picks.rationale: VST has been a medium-conviction holding all week and earns its spot as the portfolio's AI-power infrastructure expression within utilities — a sector co-leading alongside energy and industrials per the sector rotation scorecard. As data centers consuming AI workloads require 24/7 reliable baseload power, Vistra's nuclear and natural gas fleet commands premium pricing power. Tepper added +114% to his VST position in Q1 2026, making it one of his largest adds — institutional validation of the AI-power thesis at scale. U.S. government elevation of electric grid infrastructure as a national defense priority under the Defense Production Act is a structural positive.
picks.catalyst: Federal grid infrastructure policy announcement or new data center power purchase agreement (PPA) signed with a hyperscaler in Q2/Q3 2026
picks.entryNote: Held at medium conviction given the sector's 'Tier 2' classification in the rotation scorecard; Tepper's +114% add and Defense Production Act elevation validate the thesis. Utilities sector is flagged as a leader in the May 20 update alongside energy and industrials.
picks.exitTrigger: Nuclear plant regulatory complications halt planned capacity expansions, or hyperscaler data center buildout slows materially reducing contracted power demand
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory and licensing delays for nuclear capacity expansion combined with a macro demand shock reducing power demand from industrial users

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: GLD remains the highest-conviction defensive holding — a 40%+ total return over the past year confirms its role as the portfolio's tail-risk and dollar-debasement shock absorber. In the current regime of Moody's US sovereign downgrade, negative equity risk premium, fiscal deficits above 6% of GDP, and geopolitical uncertainty from the Hormuz closure, gold is the canonical multi-factor hedge. Central bank purchases are running above prior estimates in 2026, and the Warsh Fed transition introduces additional policy uncertainty that historically benefits gold. Zero credit risk, near-zero equity correlation in genuine risk-off.
picks.catalyst: Any hot PCE print above 2.9% (due ~May 30) reconfirming stagflation thesis; Moody's downgrade follow-through in credit markets; Middle East escalation
picks.entryNote: GLD had a 40.05% total return over the past year; central bank demand rising above estimates in H1 2026. Not cheap on an absolute basis but the macro regime fully supports continued appreciation. Carry cost is the primary drag vs. Treasuries.
picks.exitTrigger: Hormuz reopening triggers a deflationary shock, Fed pivots definitively dovish and real yields collapse, or a strong dollar episode above DXY 110 crimps gold in USD terms
picks.keyRisk: A deflationary resolution to the Middle East conflict combined with a sharp growth slowdown causes rapid dollar strength, suppressing gold prices

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD is the portfolio's core dividend quality anchor — it has been outperforming the S&P 500 YTD with robust inflows exceeding $15B as investors rotate into 'hard hat' sectors and dividend growers. In a late-cycle stagflationary regime, SCHD's tilt toward consumer staples, healthcare, and industrials with proven dividend growth provides genuine defensiveness. Its 0.06% expense ratio and ~3.5-4% yield make it a compelling alternative to Treasuries given the negative equity risk premium environment. The 2026 reconstitution added higher dividend growth stocks, improving its inflation-fighting quality.
picks.catalyst: Stagflation signal intensification driving further defensive rotation; any market sell-off accelerates SCHD's relative outperformance vs. growth names
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; intensifies if May 30 PCE print is hot
picks.entryNote: Outperforming the S&P 500 YTD on defensive rotation; low beta (~0.7) provides natural downside protection. $15B+ in recent inflows signal durable institutional demand. At current yields, competes favorably with short-duration bonds on total return basis.
picks.exitTrigger: Fed pivots sharply dovish and growth stocks resume leadership with the S&P 500 up 20%+ in a risk-on melt-up, where SCHD's defensive positioning becomes an opportunity cost
picks.keyRisk: A sharp pro-cyclical pivot where high-growth equities reclaim leadership would cause SCHD to underperform meaningfully on a relative basis

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: BRK.B has been a top-3 defensive name all week and the thesis is unimpeachable in the current macro regime. With $397.4B in cash and T-bills — the largest in Berkshire's history at ~59% of its combined cash and equity portfolio — Berkshire is structurally positioned to benefit from a market dislocation: its cash is both earning 4-5% T-bill yields and serving as offensive dry powder for acquisitions at distressed prices. The diversified business mix (insurance float funding, railroads, energy, consumer brands) provides natural inflation protection. Greg Abel's first major tech bet (GOOGL +204%) signals a disciplined generational transition.
picks.catalyst: A market sell-off scenario in which Berkshire deploys its $397B cash hoard into large acquisitions at attractive prices — the 'Buffett put' effect
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; accelerates in any risk-off regime
picks.entryNote: Trades at ~1.5-1.6x book — reasonable for a company that is net long the current rising rate and inflation environment through insurance float and energy assets. Acts as the portfolio's 'sleep at night' compounder with historical outperformance in risk-off environments.
picks.exitTrigger: Berkshire deploys its cash at clearly overvalued prices (transformative acquisition at 30x+ EBITDA), destroying the capital discipline that justifies the premium
picks.keyRisk: Greg Abel transition introduces unexpected strategy shifts or key-person risk at the subsidiary level that erodes Berkshire's decentralized operating model efficiency

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury ETF
picks.rationale: IEF is the portfolio's duration hedge against the tail scenario where stagflation resolves into a demand-destruction recession, causing the Fed to pivot dovishly and the 10-year yield to fall from 4.63% toward 3.5-4.0%. At current yields (~4.3%), IEF provides meaningful current income and negative equity correlation in a genuine risk-off move. Sized modestly (4%) because the primary macro scenario is 'higher-for-longer' (toxic for duration), but as tail insurance against a rapid growth deterioration, the asymmetry is attractive. The 30-year briefly breached 5.19% this week — a signal that long-end pain could overshoot and snap back.
picks.catalyst: A weaker-than-expected May jobs report or Q2 GDP miss that forces markets to reprice recession risk and drives a flight-to-quality into Treasuries
picks.entryNote: 10Y at 4.63%, 30Y briefly above 5.19% — yields are near multi-year highs, creating a structurally attractive entry for a tail hedge. 97.4% of CME market participants expect rates unchanged at next meeting, so any negative surprise has significant duration upside.
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year Treasury yield breaks decisively above 5.0% with inflation reaccelerating above 4.5%, indicating the stagflation scenario has fully taken hold and duration is no longer a hedge
picks.keyRisk: The primary macro scenario — higher-for-longer with a potential rate hike — is directly negative for IEF; this position is insurance, not conviction, and must be sized accordingly

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO anchors the defensive sleeve as the quintessential pricing-power staple in a stagflationary regime. With ~65% of revenue international (natural USD hedge), a sub-0.6 beta, ~3% dividend yield, and a global distribution moat that took 100+ years to build, KO provides genuine ballast in any macro scenario. Consumer Staples are up 10%+ YTD and the sector rotation scorecard flags XLP for a potential tactical upgrade if stagflation signals intensify. Berkshire owns KO at 11.6% of the disclosed equity portfolio — the longest-held Buffett conviction position in the book. Its requirement of 20+ consecutive years of dividend raises reflects the strictest capital return discipline.
picks.catalyst: Stagflation intensification driving defensive rotation into consumer staples; Q2 earnings showing continued pricing power with international volume recovery
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~23x forward earnings — not cheap in absolute terms, but justified by beta <0.6, ~3% yield, and genuine pricing moat. Consumer Staples sector flagged for potential tactical upgrade in the May 20 rotation update.
picks.exitTrigger: Organic revenue growth turns negative for two consecutive quarters as pricing power erodes under consumer trade-down to private label, or Berkshire exits the position signaling a fundamental moat reassessment
picks.keyRisk: GLP-1 drug adoption at scale durably reduces sugary beverage consumption volumes, creating a structural secular headwind to Coca-Cola's core carbonated soft drink revenue

summary: The macro regime is firmly late-cycle stagflationary overheating: 10-year yields at 4.63%, the 30-year briefly breaching 5.19% this week, CPI at 3.8% YoY, no Fed cuts priced for 2026, and a negative equity risk premium (-50bps) with the new Warsh Fed introducing additional hawkish policy uncertainty. The growth book expresses this through AI infrastructure leaders (NVDA — reported today with an 8th consecutive beat; AVGO; GOOGL with 63% Cloud growth), hard-asset and defense beneficiaries (XOM, RTX), financial cycle plays (GS, SPGI), the oral GLP-1 product cycle (LLY post-FDA Foundayo approval), AWS cloud compounding (AMZN), and AI-power infrastructure (VST). Every growth name is a carry-over from prior days except NVDA, which rotates from medium to high conviction following today's Q1 earnings confirmation; LLY holds despite the risk report's valuation flag because the Foundayo FDA approval and $2B guidance raise change the near-term fundamental picture materially. The five defensive positions — GLD (sovereign/inflation hedge), SCHD (dividend quality rotation beneficiary), BRK.B (fortress balance sheet + optionality), IEF (recession tail hedge at high yield entry), and KO (pricing-power staple) — together cover four distinct exposures and provide meaningful downside buffering in the dominant bear case. The primary risk underwritten is that the stagflation trap tightens further in H2 2026, forcing a Fed rate hike that crashes high-duration growth multiples — partially hedged through GLD, BRK.B, and the hard-asset/short-duration tilt in the growth book itself.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans six distinct GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL), Information Technology (AVGO, NVDA — two AI infrastructure names sharing secular exposure but at different value-chain points: custom ASIC silicon vs. GPU compute), Industrials (RTX), Energy (XOM), Financials (GS, SPGI — complementary within sector: capital markets vs. data/ratings duopoly), Health Care (LLY), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), and Utilities (VST) — satisfying the 5-sector minimum with no sector exceeding 2 names; the defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures: gold (GLD), broad dividend/quality income (SCHD), diversified operating conglomerate/cash fortress (BRK.B), long-duration Treasuries (IEF), and consumer staples pricing-power (KO), with no exposure doubled up.

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: GOOGL is the highest-conviction carry-over from every prior session this week. Google Cloud revenue surged +63% YoY in Q1 2026 to deliver $109.9B in total revenue (+20% YoY) with a $460B cloud backlog, while Berkshire Hathaway added +204% to its GOOGL stake in Q1 — both institutional and fundamental signals are aligned. At a 31.6% operating margin and with Alphabet aggressively funding AI expansion with ~$60B in global debt raised, the platform monetization flywheel is accelerating. The macro backdrop (steepening curve, higher rates) is a mild headwind on multiples, but the growth rate more than compensates at current valuation.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings with Google Cloud growth acceleration and further AI monetization evidence across Search and YouTube
picks.entryNote: Trading near 52-week highs after the Q1 blowout; forward P/E ~22x on 2026 consensus — reasonable for a company growing cloud at 63% YoY. GOOG also announced a $0.22/share dividend with ex-date June 8, adding income support.
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 40% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or DOJ antitrust remedy forces Search divestiture
picks.keyRisk: Antitrust ruling requiring structural remedy to Search monopoly, which would impair the core revenue engine

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: AVGO is the purest play on AI custom silicon and networking outside of NVDA, and critically, it sits at a different point in the value chain — custom ASICs and switching, not GPU training. Q1 FY2026 EPS of $2.05 beat the $1.88 estimate by 9%, with YoY EPS growth of 28%, and Zacks projects FY2027 sales +40.5% and earnings +39.5%. AVGO avoids NVDA's export control overhang on H20/Blackwell chips to China since its revenue is primarily from hyperscaler custom chip programs. Sector rotation briefing confirmed AI infrastructure spend (hyperscaler capex $485B+ in 2026) as the dominant structural tailwind.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2026 earnings with EPS consensus of $2.32 — expected sequential double-digit EPS expansion confirming AI ASIC ramp
picks.entryNote: AVGO closed ~$427 on May 15 (per Yahoo Finance data), trading near 52-week highs after its strong Q1 beat. Forward P/E ~28-30x is premium but justified by the growth runway; FCF yield remains among the highest in semis.
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of a major hyperscaler custom chip design win, or gross margin compression below 60% as AI ASIC mix shifts
picks.keyRisk: Hyperscaler capex pullback — if one or more of MSFT/AMZN/GOOG/META slows AI infrastructure spend, AVGO faces an immediate earnings revision downgrade cycle

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: META delivered the fastest quarterly revenue growth since 2021 in Q1 2026 at +33% YoY to $56.31B, beating consensus, with a jaw-dropping 81.8% gross margin and 41.3% operating margin — the best in mega-cap tech. Q2 2026 guidance of $58-61B implies continued ~25% growth. Both Berkshire Hathaway (Abel era) and Bill Ackman's Pershing Square initiated or hold META as a new position, providing strong smart money validation. In the stagflation regime, META's advertising-driven, asset-light FCF model is one of the few tech businesses that can withstand higher discount rates on earnings power alone.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings report with guidance for continued +25% revenue growth and Llama/AI monetization progress
picks.entryNote: Returned as a high-conviction position after briefly being medium-conviction mid-week. Trading at ~19x forward P/E on 2026 consensus — arguably the cheapest mega-cap tech name given its growth rate. Revisions are strongly positive post-Q1.
picks.exitTrigger: Operating margin falls below 35% due to AI capex escalation (Reality Labs or Llama infrastructure), or regulatory ad-targeting restrictions in EU/US impair monetization
picks.keyRisk: AI capex escalation eroding operating margins materially — Meta has committed to $60-65B in 2026 capex

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: LLY is the only mega-cap healthcare name that scores cleanly across growth, quality, and defensive characteristics simultaneously — a rare combination in a stagflation regime. Q1 2026 revenue came in at $19.799B beating consensus, with a 48.62% EBITDA margin reflecting exceptional pricing power in GLP-1. The stock trades near $1,005 on May 15 — well below its 52-week high of $1,133.95 — offering a re-entry at an attractive discount (~11% below ATH) after the UNH-led healthcare sector selloff dragged LLY lower. Beta of just 0.48 provides natural portfolio ballast in a high-volatility macro regime. GLP-1 global addressable market (obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular, Alzheimer's) remains the single largest secular growth story in pharma with a patent runway through ~2031.
picks.catalyst: Next earnings report August 5, 2026 — expected to show continued Zepbound/Mounjaro demand acceleration; donanemab Alzheimer's launch trajectory
picks.catalystWindow: August 5, 2026 (next 11 weeks) plus June FDA decisions on tirzepatide label expansions
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$1,005, ~11% below the $1,133.95 ATH set in January 2026. Forward P/E ~35x on FY2026 consensus is premium but supported by 20%+ revenue CAGR and near-50% EBITDA margins. Beta of 0.48 is a structural risk reducer.
picks.exitTrigger: GLP-1 safety signal emerges from FDA post-market surveillance, or a rival drug (semaglutide combo or retatrutide) unexpectedly captures outsized market share, or CMS drug pricing negotiations impair insulin/GLP-1 economics
picks.keyRisk: FDA safety signal or unexpected competitor approval that materially disrupts the GLP-1 competitive moat

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: GS is the premier capital markets and investment banking beneficiary of the current macro regime — steepened yield curve (+50bps 2s10s), re-accelerating bond issuance (high yield and investment grade both active in a volatile rate environment), and recovering M&A activity as deal conviction returns post-trade-war pause. Druckenmiller's Duquesne family office listed XLF (which is anchored by JPM, BRK.B, and GS) as its top buy in Q4 2025. Financials trade at a discount to the overall S&P 500 index, making GS one of the few growth-adjacent sectors not expensive. SPGI's Ratings revenue surprises to the upside when issuance is active — GS leads that issuance pipeline.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings in mid-July, expected to show M&A advisory revenue acceleration and trading gains from elevated volatility
picks.entryNote: Financials sector broadly trades at a discount to the S&P 500 on forward earnings. GS benefits from the steepened 2s10s spread of +50bps — every 25bps of steepening is a meaningful NIM and trading revenue tailwind for bulge-bracket capital markets firms.
picks.exitTrigger: 2s10s spread re-inverts (signaling recession), credit markets seize up (HY OAS widens above 500bps), or regulatory capital increases under Basel III Endgame impose material revenue headwinds
picks.keyRisk: Credit cycle turning — a rapid expansion of HY OAS spreads from current ~279bps toward 500bps would signal default cycle risk that impairs GS's loan book and deal pipeline simultaneously

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM is the highest-quality energy name with direct exposure to the Strait of Hormuz supply shock and crude oil at ~$102/bbl — the sector is the deepest value in the market at ~32% discount to both the S&P 500 and its own 10-year average. XOM's Q1 2026 beat was driven by its trading desk outperformance and Pioneer acquisition synergies. As an integrated major, XOM has the lowest break-even cost structure, largest refining buffer, and highest FCF yield in the peer group. Energy institutional inflows are accelerating in 2026. Berkshire trimmed CVX modestly but XOM remains the peer with superior FCF generation at current crude prices.
picks.catalyst: Strait of Hormuz resolution (bullish for shares as risk premium partly unwinds; upside $165+ consensus) or continued supply disruption (supports $100+ crude and elevated FCF)
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing through Q3 2026; next earnings report early August
picks.entryNote: Trading near $163 vs. analyst consensus target of ~$164 (MarketBeat). The stock has lagged the energy sector's YTD rally despite being the highest-quality name — offering a catch-up opportunity. Energy is the only sector at a 32% discount to both the index and its own history.
picks.exitTrigger: Crude oil price falls below $75/bbl on demand destruction (recession signal), or geopolitical resolution causes a rapid normalization of the oil risk premium below the fund's cost basis

phaseData.macro:
Here is your macro strategy briefing for Friday, May 15, 2026:

---

## 📊 US EQUITIES MACRO BRIEFING — May 15, 2026

### DATA DASHBOARD

**Fed Policy**
The effective Fed Funds rate currently sits at ~4.25–4.50% (unchanged since the last meeting). 
Treasury yields spiked sharply Friday as traders priced in interest rate policy under newly confirmed Fed Chair Kevin Warsh, who was confirmed by the Senate on Wednesday.
 
The March 2026 dot plot projected a 3.00–3.25% Fed funds rate by year-end 2027.
 However, 
markets have fully priced out any possibility of a Fed rate cut this year, while some traders are increasingly betting on a potential rate hike by December.
 
Per CME FedWatch, traders see no rate cuts in 2026 at all — reversing expectations from months ago — with Bank of America extending its first cut forecast to the second half of 2027, and JPMorgan keeping CPI above 3.0% until early 2027.


**Yield Curve**

The 10-year note finished May 15, 2026 at 4.59% and the 2-year at 4.09%, marking the highest level for each since February 2025.
 
The 30-year bond jumped nearly 11 basis points to 5.121%, the highest since May 22, 2025, and nearing the highest since October 2023.
 The 2s10s spread is a **+50 bps** positive steepener, and the 3m10y is also positive — a curve that has re-steepened sharply from prolonged inversion. Term premium is clearly widening, a bearish signal for long-duration assets.

**Inflation — RE-ACCELERATING** 🔴

In April, the CPI for all items rose 0.6% month-over-month (SA) and 3.8% over the last 12 months; core CPI (all items less food and energy) rose 0.4% MoM and 2.8% YoY.
 
PCE inflation jumped to 3.50% in March from 2.80% in February 2026.
 
Both the CPI and PPI reports released this week suggest the energy shock is pushing US inflation higher.
 
March CPI surged to 3.3% YoY — the highest since May 2024 — driven by an energy shock that pushed gasoline prices up 18.9% YoY.
 
Mounting inflationary pressures tied to the Iran war drove the 10-year above 4.5%; wholesale inflation accelerated at its fastest pace since 2022 in April.
 Supercore (core services ex-shelter) is sticky and elevated given labor market resilience.

**Growth**

The Atlanta Fed GDPNow model estimate for real GDP growth (SAAR) in Q2 2026 is 3.7% as of May 8.
 
The BEA's first estimate of Q1 real GDP growth was 2.0%, 0.8 percentage points above GDPNow's final nowcast.
 
Retail sales growth slowed in line with expectations but continued to reflect resilient consumer spending.
 Growth is holding, but the Q1 miss vs. nowcast bears watching. ISM Manufacturing re-entered contraction territory in early May per recent data, while ISM Services remains in modest expansion — a classic late-cycle bifurcation.

**Credit & Liquidity**
The DXY is at ~99.29 (slightly off its lows). HY OAS spreads have widened modestly from their tightest levels of the year (~310–330 bps, directionally wider), and IG OAS remains tight (~90–95 bps) — the market has not yet entered a meaningful risk-off repricing, but the direction is a warning. Financial conditions (Goldman FCI, Chicago Fed NFCI) remain mildly accommodative relative to history, but are tightening at the margin as long rates spike.

**Earnings Backdrop**

The S&P 500 closed at 7,398.93 on Friday May 8, marking its sixth straight weekly gain and the longest streak since 2024.
 Today's session shows the index at ~7,432 before the afternoon selloff. The S&P 500 forward P/E sits near 21–22x on 2026 consensus EPS — a premium to the 10-year average of ~18x — compressing the equity risk premium to near-zero given a 10-year at 4.59%. 
The March 2026 SEP showed a 30-basis-point increase in year-end 2026 PCE inflation (to 2.7%) and a 10-basis-point increase in 2026 real GDP growth (to 2.4%).


**Geopolitical / Event Tape**

The situation in the Middle East remains fragile and far from resolved, with Trump describing Iran's latest proposal as "unacceptable," while oil prices continue to climb amid fears of disruptions to global supply, with the Strait of Hormuz still closed.
 
A summit between Trump and Chinese President Xi ended without major agreements, including no indication that Beijing would help resolve the conflict.


---

### ¶1 — REGIME CALL: **LATE-CYCLE / TRANSITIONING TO STAGFLATIONARY STRESS**

This is a **late-cycle regime with stagflationary overtones** — not a clean soft landing. You have nominal growth that is holding (Atlanta GDPNow Q2 at 3.7%), but inflation is re-accelerating hard above 3.5% on the back of an energy/geopolitical shock (Iran/Hormuz), a new Fed chair inheriting a nearly impossible mandate, markets fully pricing out cuts, and long-end yields surging to fresh cycle highs (30y at 5.12%). The real policy rate is firmly restrictive (Fed funds ~4.35% minus headline PCE ~3.5% = ~+85 bps real), but the bond market is demanding even more compensation at the long end as term premium expands. The equity risk premium is near zero — a condition historically associated with sub-par forward equity returns.

---

### ¶2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** Energy and commodity-linked equities (direct beneficiary of the Hormuz/oil shock — XLE, OXY, CVX); short-duration value — Financials (steeper yield curve benefits net interest margin for banks; XLF outperforming); Defense & Aerospace (geopolitical premium; RTX, LMT, NOC); Healthcare (defensive earnings, relatively rate-insensitive, shelter in a stagflation storm). Real assets and inflation-linked instruments (TIPS, gold proxies, infrastructure) are also outperforming. Within the broad market, 
resilient consumer spending
 continues to support Consumer Staples selectively.

**Breaking:** Long-duration growth — mega-cap Tech and AI names whose discounted cash flow models are acutely sensitive to discount rate expansion; 
the 10-year surging to 4.595% while the 2-year moved to 4.079%
 is a violent headwind for high-multiple Nasdaq constituents. Small caps (Russell 2000) are being squeezed by higher-for-longer refinancing risk. Real estate (XLRE) is structurally impaired at 5%+ long rates. The forward P/E compression trade is live — any multiple contraction from 21–22x back toward the 10-year mean of ~18x implies ~15% downside to index levels independent of earnings risk.

---

### ¶3 — WHAT TO WATCH (Highest-Impact, 1–6 Month Horizon)

1. **April PCE (late May) + May CPI (June 10):** 
The Consumer Price Index for May 2026 is scheduled for release on June 10, 2026.
 
Investors will quickly pivot to April PCE (late May), the Fed's preferred measure, alongside May non-farm payrolls and retail sales.
 If PCE re-accelerates above 3.7–3.8%, the hike probability in futures could cross 50% — a potential shock to equity multiple assumptions across the board.

2. **Iran Nuclear Negotiations / Strait of Hormuz:** 
The Middle East remains unresolved, with the Strait of Hormuz still closed and oil prices climbing on supply disruption fears.
 A re-opening of the Strait would sharply disinflationary — cutting 50–80 bps off headline CPI trajectory — and could be the single most bullish macro event for equities over the next 6 months. Escalation, conversely, is the tail risk that breaks the cycle into outright contraction.

3. **New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's First Policy Signal / June FOMC:** 
Yields spiked Friday as traders looked to price interest rate policy under new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh.
 
While Warsh is viewed as more open to rate cuts, the latest FOMC meeting showed three voters ready to dissent over policy-statement language — suggesting a rate cut still looks unlikely without significantly softer inflation data.
 His first public communication and any June FOMC statement language adjustment will be the highest-information-content macro event for rate-sensitive assets in Q3.

---
> *All data as of market close or intraday May 15, 2026. This briefing is for informational purposes and does not constitute investment advice.*

**Relative Strength Tier: LEADER**

## 📊 US EQUITY MACRO BRIEFING — May 18, 2026

### DATA DASHBOARD

**Fed Policy**

The Fed left the federal funds rate steady at the **3.50%–3.75%** target range for a 2nd consecutive meeting in March 2026.
 
The current effective fed funds rate is **3.63%**. As of May 14, futures markets are pricing a *gentle rise* in the policy rate, with levels around **3.7%** by late 2026 and near **3.8%** by May 2027.
 That's a dramatic shift from earlier in the year: 
as per CME FedWatch, traders now see **no rate cuts in 2026 at all**, reversing expectations from a few months ago when one quarter-point cut was still in the works.
 
Traders are now fully pricing in **one rate hike by the Fed in March 2027**, with more than a 50% chance that interest rates will rise before the end of 2026.
 On the dot plot: 
the median FOMC participant still expects inflation to return to target by 2028, but core PCE projections for 2026 were revised up by 0.1 percentage point to **2.7%**.
 With neutral rate estimated near 3.0%, the real policy rate (3.63% minus ~2.7% core PCE) sits at roughly **+95bps** — meaningfully restrictive.

**Yield Curve**

The 10-year Treasury yield climbed to around **4.63%** on Monday, reaching its highest level since January 2025, as mounting inflationary pressures tied to the Middle East conflict fueled expectations of a Fed rate hike.
 The 30-year was near **4.88%** as of the March FOMC date and has backed up further since. 
The 10Y rose above 4.6% on Monday.
 The 2-year, tracking the funds rate closely, sits near **4.00–4.10%**, leaving the **2s10s spread positively sloped at roughly +50–55bps** — a dramatic normalization from its deep inversion of 2022–2024. 
The normalization of the curve is often a more immediate warning than the initial inversion
 — a key watch item. Term premium is **rising**, driven by inflation uncertainty and fiscal concerns.

**Inflation**

In April, the CPI for All Urban Consumers rose **0.6% MoM** (seasonally adjusted) and **3.8% YoY** — the highest in nearly three years.
 
Core CPI rose to **2.8% YoY** in April, up from 2.6% in March, slightly above market expectations — the sharpest core rate since September 2025.
 
Services ex-energy rose 3.3%, with shelter at 3.3% and transportation services at 4.3%.
 Trajectory is clearly **re-accelerating**: 
March CPI had already shot to +3.3% YoY on an energy shock that pushed gasoline prices up 18.9% YoY.
 
Producer prices rose **6.0%** for the 12 months ended April
 — a leading indicator of further pass-through pressure. The FOMC's preferred measure, core PCE, is projected at **2.7%** by year-end. 
The Fed projects PCE inflation to end 2026 at 2.7%, 30bps higher than the December 2025 projection.


**Growth**

The Atlanta Fed GDPNow tracker for Q2 2026 jumped to **4.0%** after the May 14 retail sales and import/export price releases
, up from an initial 3.7% estimate. 
The BEA's latest Q1 2026 GDP estimate came in at **+2.0%** (SAAR).
 
Hiring has picked up from near-stagnant rates in 2025, and businesses are generating robust profit growth.
 
JPMorgan keeps CPI above 3.0% until early 2027, while Bank of America extended its first rate cut call to the second half of 2027.


**Credit & Liquidity**
HY OAS (ICE BofA) has widened materially from early-2026 tights but remains in the **350–380bps** range — elevated vs. January lows but not yet in stress territory. IG OAS is approximately **100–110bps**. 
The latest CPI and PPI reports prompted traders to completely rule out Fed rate cuts this year while increasing bets on a potential rate hike before year-end
 — a net financial conditions tightening. 
Oil prices continued to climb amid fears of supply disruptions, with the Strait of Hormuz still closed.
 DXY remains elevated in the **103–105** range, reflecting rate-hike repricing.

**Earnings Backdrop**

S&P 500 forward earnings yield (as of the most recent forward estimate) stands near **~4.1%**, implying a forward P/E near **~24x**
 — roughly 2–3 turns above the 10-year average. 
If the earnings yield is less than the 10-year Treasury yield, stocks may be considered overvalued.
 With the 10Y at 4.63% and earnings yield at ~4.1%, the **equity risk premium is negative** — stocks offer less yield than Treasuries. 
However, businesses are generating robust profit growth
, with Q1 earnings season broadly beating estimates.

---

### ¶1 — REGIME CALL

**Late-Cycle / Stagflationary Overheating.** The US macro regime has shifted into a **late-cycle, stagflationary overheating** print: 
both CPI and PPI data confirmed the energy shock is pushing US inflation higher, prompting traders to completely rule out rate cuts this year and increasing bets on a potential rate hike before year-end
, while simultaneously 
the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow is tracking Q2 growth at 3.7–4.0%
 — a toxic mix of above-trend nominal growth and re-accelerating prices that historically compresses equity multiples, forces the Fed's hand to the hawkish side, and ends cycles through demand destruction or financial accident.

---

### ¶2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** Energy and hard assets are the regime's clear beneficiaries — 
oil prices continued to climb amid fears of disruptions to global supply, with the Strait of Hormuz still closed
, powering the Energy sector and commodity producers. Financials benefit from a steeper yield curve (2s10s now positive ~+50bps) and NIM expansion. Value/cyclical names with pricing power — Industrials tied to defense and infrastructure, Materials — hold up in a nominal-growth-still-running environment. Short-duration equities outperform vs. long-duration growth. TIPS provide real return in a re-accelerating inflation backdrop. **Breaking:** High-duration growth (mega-cap tech, unprofitable tech) faces multiple compression as the 10Y hits 4.63%+; 
traders are fully pricing in one rate hike by March 2027 with a >50% chance before year-end 2026
, which is toxic for high-P/E names. 
Core CPI rose 0.4% MoM, the sharpest increase since January 2025
 — services stickiness is squeezing consumer discretionary and rate-sensitive REITs. Utilities underperform as real yields spike. The **negative equity risk premium** (~-50bps, earnings yield below 10Y) means bonds are now a genuine competitor to equities for the first time in this cycle, pressuring the overall market P/E ceiling.

---

### ¶3 — WHAT TO WATCH (Top 2–3 Catalysts, 1–6 Month Horizon)

**① April PCE / Core PCE Print (due ~May 30):** 
April PCE is due out around May 30, and investors will be analyzing it to gauge if the energy shock is expanding into services consumption.
 A core PCE above 2.9% would virtually seal a hike bias at the June FOMC meeting — the single highest-impact data point in the near-term window.

**② Middle East / Strait of Hormuz Resolution:** 
The situation in the Middle East remains fragile, with the Strait of Hormuz still closed and oil prices continuing to climb. A Trump-Xi summit ended without agreements to resolve the conflict.
 
Trump warned Iran to "get moving" or face consequences after departing China without progress toward ending the conflict.
 A Hormuz reopening would be a deflationary shock — reversing the energy-driven CPI spike and almost immediately repricing rate hike odds. Conversely, escalation (strikes on Iranian infrastructure) risks a $20–30/bbl crude spike and forces the Fed into an impossible dual-mandate bind.

**③ New Fed Chair Confirmation / June FOMC (June 16–17):** 
The Justice Department has halted its criminal investigation into Powell, removing a key obstacle to the Senate confirmation of his nominated successor, Kevin Warsh, with the appointment set for May 15.
 A Warsh Fed is widely perceived as more hawkish than Powell's FOMC. Combined with the June SEP (updated dot plot), this meeting carries the potential to formally introduce a **rate hike bias** into the dots for the first time since 2023 — a regime-defining event for equity risk premia across all sectors.
